claims.claim: Malaysia's Gig Workers Bill 2025, passed by Parliament in August-September 2025
claims.source: Bernama, 'Dewan Negara Passes Gig Workers' Bill 2025', 9 September 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2465712; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ for Akta Pekerja Gig 2025, lines 65-123, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
claims.correction: Use exact sequence: Dewan Rakyat passed it on 28 August 2025 and Dewan Negara passed it on 9 September 2025.
claims.stale_check: Not stale for parliamentary passage, but the Bill later became Act 872 after Royal Assent on 16 December 2025 and gazettement on 31 December 2025.

claims.claim: The Dewan Negara passed the Gig Workers Bill 2025 on 9 September 2025.
claims.source: Bernama, 'Dewan Negara Passes Gig Workers' Bill 2025', 9 September 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2465712

claims.claim: The Dewan Rakyat passed the Gig Workers Bill 2025 in August 2025.
claims.source: Bernama, 'Gig Workers Bill Deems Verbal Contracts Valid, Covers Entertainment Industry', 8 September 2025, states it was passed by Dewan Rakyat on 28 August 2025, https://bernama.com/en/region/news.php/news.php?id=2465196

claims.claim: The law brings ~1.2 million platform workers into SOCSO accident protection.
claims.source: MOHR Gig Workers Bill text D.R. 27/2025, sections 81-83, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 27-48 and 1535-1657, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf; Bernama, 31 March 2026, says Act 872 benefits about 1.64 million, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2539439
claims.correction: The 1.2 million figure was used during passage in September 2025. MOHR's later FAQ cites MDEC 2024 data of about 1.64 million people in the gig economy, and Act 872's mandatory social-security duties apply specifically to platform providers. PERKESO data also shows 821,456 self-employed contributors under Act 789 as of 5 August 2025, so the framing that all are newly brought in is overstated.
claims.stale_check: Stale: by 31 March 2026 official reporting used about 1.64 million, not 1.2 million.

claims.claim: Malaysia's first gig-worker law brings ~1.2 million riders and freelancers into SOCSO accident protection, for the first time.
claims.source: MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 38-48, states 821,456 self-employed persons had already contributed under the Self-Employment Social Security Act 2017 as of 5 August 2025, including p-hailing and e-hailing contributors, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf; Act 872 sections 81-83, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf
claims.correction: It is fair to say Act 872 creates Malaysia's first dedicated gig-worker legal framework, but not that SOCSO protection reaches gig workers for the first time. Self-employed social-security coverage already existed under Act 789.
claims.stale_check: Stale and overbroad: later official data uses 1.64 million and confirms pre-existing Act 789 coverage.

claims.claim: A 1.25% per-ride deduction funds the SOCSO contribution.
claims.source: P.U. (A) 143/2026, Gig Workers (Social Security) Regulations 2026, regulation 2, states the contribution deduction percentage is 1.25%, https://www.jtksabah.gov.my/utama/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/PUA143_2026.pdf; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 1573-1589, says deduction is for each transaction, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
claims.correction: Replace 'per-ride' with 'per transaction' or 'from gig-worker income for each transaction'; per-ride is too narrow because the Act covers services beyond ride delivery.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; the official 2026 regulation confirms 1.25%.

claims.claim: Mandatory SOCSO accident and disability protection: platforms must register workers and remit contributions on their behalf.
claims.source: Act 872/D.R. 27/2025 sections 82-83, lines 1308-1338, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 1535-1580, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
claims.correction: Clarify that these social-security obligations are imposed on platform providers, not every contracting entity.

claims.claim: Coverage spans e-hailing, p-hailing, freelancers and digital content creators.
claims.source: MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 27-35 and 235-248, says MDEC estimated 1.64 million people in p-hailing, e-hailing, freelancers, online services and others, and that the Act covers all gig workers through digital intermediary systems plus listed non-platform services, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf; Act 872 definition of gig worker, lines 195-206, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf
claims.correction: Use the official categories: p-hailing, e-hailing, freelancers, online services and other platform-mediated services, plus scheduled non-platform services. I could not verify 'digital content creators' as an explicit official category in the Act or MOHR FAQ.
claims.stale_check: Not stale, but the category wording is unsupported.

claims.claim: Grab, Foodpanda and other platforms must provide written terms.
claims.source: Act 872/D.R. 27/2025 section 8, lines 283-323, gives workers rights to be informed of agreed service-agreement terms, income details and platform automated systems; section 3 governs service agreements, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf
claims.correction: The Act requires gig workers to be informed of agreed terms and service details, but the named companies Grab and Foodpanda are not named in the Act. If naming them, phrase as examples of platform providers only if independently sourced.

claims.claim: EPF retirement contributions are NOT included in this first phase.
claims.source: Act 872/D.R. 27/2025 sections 78-83 mention SOCSO/PERKESO and Act 789, not EPF/KWSP, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf; EPF i-Saraan page describes i-Saraan as voluntary for self-employed and gig workers, https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/member/savings/i-saraan
claims.correction: State that Act 872 does not impose mandatory EPF/KWSP contributions; self-employed and gig workers may still contribute voluntarily through i-Saraan.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found of a later amendment adding mandatory EPF coverage as of 17 May 2026.

claims.claim: Voluntary i-Saraan covered ~10.7% of self-employed workers by 2022.
claims.source: EPF/KWSP news, 3 March 2023, says i-Saraan participants increased to 291,743 in 2022, https://wip-uat.epf.gov.my/news-3/3/2023; DOSM April 2022 labour force release says own-account workers were 2.72 million, https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/key-statistics-of-labour-force-in-malaysia-april-2022; World Bank working paper reports i-Saraan covered 10.7% of self-employed workers in 2022, https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/79246c20-3eef-41c4-9b89-29fcc0d35293/download
claims.correction: The percentage can be replicated from official EPF and DOSM figures if 'self-employed' is proxied by own-account workers. Clarify the denominator, because unpaid family workers may or may not be included depending on methodology.
claims.stale_check: Stale as a current coverage indicator: EPF reported 382,983 i-Saraan participants in 2023 and Bernama reported 529,667 members receiving i-Saraan incentives in 2024.

claims.claim: The new mandatory scheme does not extend to retirement.
claims.source: Act 872/D.R. 27/2025 social-security provisions refer to SOCSO/PERKESO and Self-Employment Social Security Act 2017, not EPF/KWSP, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf; EPF i-Saraan is voluntary, https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/member/savings/i-saraan
claims.correction: Clarify that the mandatory scheme is social-security accident protection, while retirement saving remains voluntary through EPF i-Saraan unless covered by other employment arrangements.
claims.stale_check: No later official source found changing this before 17 May 2026.

claims.claim: Of Malaysia's 16.7 million labour force in Q1 2025, about 3.45 million work informally.
claims.source: DOSM Principal Statistics of Labour Force, Malaysia, Q1 2025 says labour force was 17.23 million and employed persons were 16.70 million, https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/principal-statistics-of-labour-force-malaysia-first-quarter-2025; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 49-55, repeats 16.7 million as 'guna tenaga' (employment), not labour force, and 3.45 million informal employment, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf; DOSM informal report for 2023 says informal employment including agriculture was 3.45 million, https://www.dosm.gov.my/uploads/release-content/file_20250804150512.pdf
claims.correction: Correct to: In Q1 2025 Malaysia had 17.23 million in the labour force and 16.70 million employed persons. The 3.45 million informal-employment figure comes from DOSM's 2023 informal-sector/informal-employment report, not Q1 2025 labour force.
claims.stale_check: Not just stale; it confuses labour force with employed persons and mixes Q1 2025 with a 2023 informal-employment figure.

claims.claim: Gig workers are a subset of informal employment.
claims.source: MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 49-55, says informal employment includes gig work, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf; DOSM 2023 informal-sector/informal-employment report uses ILO informal-employment methodology, https://www.dosm.gov.my/uploads/release-content/file_20241128112611.pdf
claims.correction: Better: DOSM/MOHR describe gig work as included within the broader informal-employment universe, but platform gig workers may not map perfectly onto the statistical definition of informal employment.

claims.claim: DOSM and ILO sources put the gig-worker count at ~1.2 million.
claims.source: MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 27-35, cites MDEC 2024 estimate of about 1.64 million gig-economy participants, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf; Bernama/KESUMA Sept 2025 cites over 1.2 million expected beneficiaries, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2465735
claims.correction: I found official/MOHR and Bernama/KESUMA sources for 'over 1.2 million' during passage and '1.64 million' in later MOHR FAQ, but not a primary DOSM or ILO source independently putting the gig-worker count at 1.2 million.
claims.stale_check: Stale if used as current figure: official 2026 implementation reporting uses about 1.64 million.

claims.claim: The Bill's coverage figure tracks the same population.
claims.source: MOHR/KESUMA FAQ cites MDEC 2024 gig-economy estimate of 1.64 million and PERKESO Act 789 contributor data of 821,456 as of 5 August 2025, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
claims.correction: The analysis should not assert equivalence between informal employment, DOSM/ILO data and Bill coverage without a methodology note. The official figures refer to different universes: labour-force employment, informal employment, MDEC gig-economy participants and PERKESO contributors.

claims.claim: The 'no protection' framing is now obsolete.
claims.source: Act 872 sections 8, 81-83 establish statutory rights and platform-provider social-security duties, https://www.mohr.gov.my/ruupekerjagig/assets/images/ruu/D.R.272025%20RUU%20PEKERJA%20GIG%202025.pdf; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 1563-1572, states social-security duties under the Act apply only to platform providers while non-platform contracting entities' workers are encouraged to contribute under Act 789, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
claims.correction: The Act materially improves protection, but 'no protection is obsolete' is too broad because mandatory social-security duties apply only to platform providers and implementation/integration periods remain relevant.
claims.stale_check: As of 31 March 2026 the Act is in force, but some platform integration was reported as taking three to six months.

claims.source: MOHR/KESUMA FAQ, lines 1563-1572, states social-security duties under Act 872 apply only to platform providers; lines 1633-1638 state all platforms must deduct contributions for each transaction, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
claims.correction: Say 'Phase 1 creates mandatory platform-based deductions for SOCSO self-employment social-security coverage' rather than 'closed the accident gap.' The latter overstates completeness.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; the limitation is in the official FAQ.

claims.claim: The Phase 2 question is whether EPF mandatory contributions reach platform workers, and which government dares to legislate it.
claims.source: No primary source found establishing a formal 'Phase 2' policy agenda for mandatory EPF contributions for platform workers. Act 872 contains no EPF/KWSP mandatory contribution provision; EPF i-Saraan remains voluntary, https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/member/savings/i-saraan
claims.correction: Frame this as analysis or policy speculation, not fact. There is evidence of an EPF gap, but not of an official Phase 2 EPF legislative plan.
claims.stale_check: No later official Phase 2 EPF source found before 17 May 2026.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysia's first gig-worker law brings ~1.2 million riders and freelancers into SOCSO accident protection, for the first time.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: SOCSO/PERKESO self-employed coverage existed under Act 789 before Act 872, and MOHR says 821,456 self-employed persons had contributed by 5 August 2025. The 'for the first time' wording is only defensible for a dedicated gig-worker legal framework, not SOCSO coverage itself.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: A 1.25% per-ride deduction funds the SOCSO contribution.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Official regulations and FAQ state a 1.25% deduction per transaction/from income, not specifically per ride. 'Per-ride' narrows a multi-sector law to ride/delivery work.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: DOSM and ILO sources put the gig-worker count at ~1.2 million
cardinal_sin_flags.why: I found official/KESUMA and Bernama reporting for over 1.2 million during passage and MOHR/MDEC's later 1.64 million estimate, but not a primary DOSM or ILO source independently giving 1.2 million.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Coverage spans e-hailing, p-hailing, freelancers and digital content creators.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Official FAQ verifies p-hailing, e-hailing, freelancers and online services, but I could not verify 'digital content creators' as an explicit covered category in the Act or FAQ.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Of Malaysia's 16.7 million labour force in Q1 2025, about 3.45 million work informally
cardinal_sin_flags.why: DOSM says Q1 2025 labour force was 17.23 million; 16.70 million was employed persons. The 3.45 million informal-employment number is from a 2023 informal-employment report, not Q1 2025 labour force.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: DOSM Principal Statistics of Labour Force Q1 2025, https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/principal-statistics-of-labour-force-malaysia-first-quarter-2025; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ lines 49-55, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.why: The Act establishes mandatory platform-provider deductions, but MOHR says social-security duties under Act 872 apply only to platform providers; non-platform gig workers are encouraged to contribute under Act 789. 'Closed' overstates coverage completeness.

omitted_facts: Act 872 received Royal Assent on 16 December 2025 and was gazetted on 31 December 2025; Bernama citing KESUMA, 31 December 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2507749

Act 872 came into force on 31 March 2026 and official reporting then used about 1.64 million gig-sector individuals, not 1.2 million; Bernama, 31 March 2026, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2539439

MOHR's FAQ cites MDEC 2024 data estimating about 1.64 million gig-economy participants and 246 registered platforms; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ lines 27-37, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf

MOHR's FAQ says 821,456 self-employed persons had contributed under the Self-Employment Social Security Act 2017 as of 5 August 2025, including 133,481 p-hailing and 189,450 e-hailing contributors; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ lines 38-48, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf

Act 872's social-security duties apply to platform providers; non-platform contracting entities do not have the same Act 872 social-security obligation and workers are encouraged to contribute under Act 789; MOHR/KESUMA FAQ lines 1563-1572, https://www.mohr.gov.my/aktapekerjagig2025/assets/documents/faq.pdf
P.U. (A) 143/2026 made the 1.25% social-security deduction rate legally operative from 31 March 2026; https://www.jtksabah.gov.my/utama/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/PUA143_2026.pdf

DOSM Q1 2025 labour force was 17.23 million, while employed persons were 16.70 million; DOSM Q1 2025 release, https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/principal-statistics-of-labour-force-malaysia-first-quarter-2025
source_assessment: The original analysis lists no sources, so source diversity is effectively zero. It appears to rely on official-government style figures, but does not cite KESUMA/MOHR, Parliament, DOSM, PERKESO, EPF/KWSP, MDEC, platform companies, worker associations, unions, employers, opposition MPs, academics or legal experts. The absence of citations creates several avoidable errors: stale 1.2 million coverage figure, labour-force/employment confusion, and unsupported attribution to DOSM/ILO.
overall_assessment: The analysis is directionally right that Act 872 creates a new gig-worker framework with mandatory platform-based PERKESO/SOCSO deductions and no mandatory EPF retirement contribution. However, it contains material precision problems: it uses a stale 1.2 million figure where current official implementation sources use about 1.64 million, overstates 'first time' SOCSO coverage despite pre-existing Act 789 self-employed coverage, mislabels employed persons as labour force, and presents several policy conclusions as settled facts. It is usable only after correcting the numbers, scope limits and stale framing.
